turns.text: Hello Eugene, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're getting closer to your due date, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s fine. I suppose talking about it is better than just sitting here staring at the peeling paint in the kitchen, waiting for the next kick. 

I’m tired. Honestly, I’m beyond tired—it’s a bone-deep exhaustion that doesn’t go away even when I manage to get a few hours of sleep. Climbing those stairs to our apartment feels like a marathon every single day, and then there’s the constant worry about money. It’s always there, humming in the background.

My midwife, she’s decent enough, I guess. But every time I walk into that clinic, I feel like I’m just a number on a page, a "medium risk" file they need to clear out before the next one comes in. When they look at my chart—seven pregnancies—and then look at me, at my worn-out shoes and my tired face, I can’t help but wonder what they’re thinking. Do they think I have too much on my plate? Do they look at my life and judge it? 

I’m just trying to get this baby here safely. That’s all that matters. But now with this GBS talk, my head is spinning. It’s just one more thing I can't control, another "what if" keeping me up at night. I just want this one to be okay. So, yeah, I’m ready to talk. Where do you want to start?

When you think about your goals for your antenatal care during pregnancy, what did you most want from your interactions with midwives, doctors, and the wider care system — and to what extent were those goals met?

Honestly? I just wanted to feel like a person, not a textbook case. When you’ve been through this as many times as I have, you start to feel invisible. I wanted someone to look me in the eye and see the human being behind the "seven pregnancies" statistic—someone who understands that just because I’ve done this before doesn't mean I’m not scared to death this time around. 

I was looking for a little bit of grace. When money is tight and you’re working full-time while trying to keep a house running, you’re already stretched so thin. I needed them to acknowledge how hard it is to actually *make* it to these appointments, let alone follow all their instructions. 

Have they met those goals? It’s hit or miss. Some of the midwives are kind, and they take the time to listen when my voice shakes a bit about the GBS swab or the labor logistics. But then there are days where the system just feels cold, like they’re rushing to check boxes on a form so they can move on to the next patient. I don't need them to be my best friend, but I do need to feel like they care about whether me and this baby actually make it to the finish line in one piece. Does that make sense?

When you think about how you sought and used information during your pregnancy — from healthcare providers, online sources, apps, books, and people around you — how did you decide what to trust and act upon?

Honestly, I try to stay away from the internet. If I start Googling things—especially about infections or "what if" scenarios—I’ll just end up in a spiral of panic, and I don't have the energy for that. My brain already runs through enough nightmare scenarios at three in the morning without reading horror stories from strangers on forums.

Mostly, I rely on my midwife. She’s the one who’s actually seen me, heard the baby’s heartbeat, and knows my history. I figure if there’s something serious, she’ll tell me. That doesn't mean I don't have my guard up, though. When she talks about things like the GBS test, I listen carefully, but I’m filtering it through my own reality. I have to think: *Can I actually follow that advice given my life?* 

If someone tells me to "just take it easy," I have to laugh, or I’d cry. I don't have the luxury of putting my feet up. So, I take the medical advice, but I’m the one who decides how to make it fit into my day-to-day survival. I trust the science, sure, but I trust my own gut more. I’ve delivered six babies; I know when something feels off. I just hope that this time, my gut and the doctors are on the same page.
Which sources did you find most useful, and what made them trustworthy in your eyes?

It’s funny, because even though I’m a bit wary of the clinic, the midwife is still the only one I really trust for the technical stuff. It’s not just about the medical degree; it’s about having a face to connect to the information. When she looks at me and explains the GBS, I can see if she’s being honest or if she’s just reading from a script. That human connection—even if it’s fleeting—makes a world of difference. 

Honestly, I think I trust other women more than anything else. Not the forums online, but the women I know, like my neighbor. She’s been through the ringer too, and she doesn't use those fancy clinical words that make me feel like I’m back in school. When she talks about labor, or about managing the exhaustion, I know she’s speaking from experience, not a textbook. She understands the "real" side of it—the stuff the doctors don't mention, like how much you worry about being able to afford the ride to the hospital, or how you manage to keep the house running while your back is screaming.

That’s what makes a source trustworthy to me: knowing that they aren't judging my life, but rather, they're living in the same world I am. If they haven't been where I am, it’s hard to really listen to what they have to say.

When you think about the emotional landscape of your pregnancy — the highs, the anxieties, the uncertainties — what feelings were most prominent, and how well did your care environment support you in managing them?

It’s a strange mix. There’s this quiet, fierce joy—sometimes, when I’m lying still and I feel that little nudge from inside, I just close my eyes and feel so lucky. It’s a miracle, really, after all the noise and the bills and the long shifts. But that joy is almost always tethered to this heavy, cold weight of anxiety. 

The care environment? It’s not really set up for the "emotional landscape," is it? They’re busy checking my blood pressure and listening for the heart rate—which I’m grateful for, don’t get me wrong—but there’s no room for the rest of it. When I’ve tried to hint at how much the stress of the stairs or the fear about the GBS results is eating me up, the response is usually, "Well, try to rest more," or "You’re doing just fine." 

It feels like they want to keep the conversation strictly on the physical. They don’t see that my anxiety *is* part of my health. When I walk out of that clinic, I feel like I’ve dropped off a lab sample but left the heavy stuff behind, still sitting in my chest. I’m managing it mostly on my own, which is isolating. I need a space where it's okay to admit I’m scared without feeling like I'm wasting their time.
Can you describe a period during your pregnancy when you felt most emotionally unsupported — what was happening and what would have helped?

It was just last week. I had gone in for a routine check-up, and I was feeling particularly overwhelmed—the kind of day where the stairs felt like a mountain and I’d been up half the night worrying about the GBS screening and how we’d manage if I ended up needing a longer hospital stay. I’m a mess, right?

I tried to tell the midwife that I was really struggling with the anxiety of it all, not just the physical stuff. She was mid-charting, barely looking up, and she gave me one of those practiced, sympathetic smiles—you know the one—and said, "Seven babies, you’re a pro at this, Eugenie. You know the drill, you’ll be fine."

That "you’re a pro" comment felt like a slap. It dismissed everything I was feeling as if I didn’t have a right to be scared because I’d done it before. Like my history makes me immune to the fear. I felt so small. What would have helped? I just needed her to stop typing, turn the chair around, and say, "That sounds incredibly heavy. Tell me what’s the biggest thing keeping you up at night." Just acknowledging that this seventh time is just as real and just as terrifying as the first would have been enough. I just needed to be seen.

When you think about the decisions you faced during pregnancy — including screening tests, monitoring choices, and care options — how did you go about making those decisions, and who or what most influenced them?

Most of the time, I feel like the decisions are already made for me. They present it like it’s a choice, but really, it’s just the path of least resistance. With the GBS screening, for example, the midwife talks about it as a standard procedure—a "routine" thing. When you're in my position, you don't really say "no" to the routine stuff, because you don't want to be the difficult patient. You want to be the one who does everything right so that *if* something goes wrong, you know you did your part.

My husband and I talk it over at night, usually when we’re both too tired to really think straight. We look at our finances and think about the "what ifs." We weigh whether a test or a procedure is going to cause more stress or save us from a bigger crisis later. 

Mostly, it’s fear that influences me. I’m scared of the infection, so I’ll agree to the swab. I’m scared of the labor going too fast, so I listen when they talk about the hospital plan. It’s not really about picking the "best" care; it’s about picking the path that keeps me and the baby safe while trying not to lose my mind—or my paycheck—in the process. I just want to make sure I’m not signing up for something that’s going to make the birth more complicated than it needs to be.
Can you walk me through a specific decision point — what were the options, how did you weigh them, and what did you ultimately choose?

The GBS screening really has me stuck. My midwife laid it out: the swab, then the possibility of that "positive" label, which would mean I have to be at the hospital for enough time to get the IV antibiotics before the baby is born. 

The "option" felt like a trap. If I say no to the swab, I’m "declining standard care," which makes me feel like a bad mom if something goes wrong. If I say yes, I risk that positive result, which complicates everything. My biggest fear isn't the test; it’s the logistics. I’m thinking about that commute—the bus, the walk, the traffic in Somerville—and how that could eat up the four hours of medicine time I’d need. If I show up and haven't had the full dose, does that mean they’ll hold me longer? Or worse, does it mean they’ll panic and take the baby to the NICU?

I weighed it against the risk of the infection itself—that’s the one thing I can't live with. I’m choosing to go through with the swab. I feel like I have to. The "weighing" wasn't really a choice between health and non-health; it was a choice between two different kinds of stress. I picked the one that keeps the baby safest, even if it makes my life a logistical nightmare. It feels like I’m choosing for the baby, not for myself.
When you think about the physical changes in your body during pregnancy, how did those changes affect how you felt about yourself — and were those feelings acknowledged or addressed within your care?

It’s strange. With my first, I felt like I was glowing, you know? But with this seventh one, my body just feels like a worn-out machine that’s being pushed way past its limit. Every day, I look in the mirror and I don’t recognize the person staring back. My ankles are swollen, my back aches, and I’m just so damn heavy. It’s hard to feel like "me" when I’m constantly struggling to just breathe or climb those stairs to our apartment.

The care team? They see the physical changes, but they only see them as data points. They measure the bump, they listen to the weight, they check the swelling. They don't ever ask, "How are you feeling in your own skin?" 

Sometimes I feel like my body is just a vessel for the baby, and that I’m being left behind. I miss being able to move without pain, and I miss feeling like I have some strength left in me. I wanted a provider to say, "I know your body has been through so much, and it’s okay that you’re feeling worn down." Instead, it’s all "everything looks normal" and "you’re doing great." It feels dismissive. My body is doing a massive amount of work, and it feels like the providers only notice if something goes wrong, rather than seeing the toll it takes on me every single day.
Were there aspects of your physical transformation that felt unexpected, difficult, or that nobody had prepared you for?

It’s the sheer, relentless *exhaustion* this time. Nobody tells you that after six, your body just doesn't bounce back the same way. People act like because I’ve been through pregnancy so many times, I’m some kind of expert, like it’s just a routine I can do in my sleep. But this pregnancy feels different—my muscles feel weaker, the Braxton Hicks contractions are stronger and more frequent, and that deep, heavy ache in my hips... it’s constant.

Nobody prepared me for how lonely that physical toll can be when you’re already trying to keep a house going. With the first, everyone asks how you’re feeling. By the seventh, people just see the bump and make a comment about how "full" your hands must be. It’s like they assume I’m used to the pain, so it doesn't matter anymore. 

The most unexpected part is the fear that my body won't hold up. That my heart or my lungs or my legs will just give out before I make it to the delivery room. I feel like I’m walking around with a ticking clock in my belly, and my own body feels like it's betraying me by being so tired. It’s hard to stay positive when every move is a struggle and you feel like nobody really sees the heavy lifting you're actually doing.

When you think about the informal support you received during pregnancy — from partners, family, friends, or online communities — how did that support compare to what you received from healthcare professionals, and what role did each play?

My husband and my neighbor are the ones actually in the trenches with me. My husband, he’s tired too—he’s working his tail off just like I am—but when he rubs my feet at night or helps me navigate those stairs without a word, that’s the kind of support that keeps me upright. It’s practical, grounded, and silent. He’s the one who shares the weight of the worry. When I talk about the GBS test and the hospital commute, he doesn’t tell me to "relax"; he starts calculating how we’ll get a ride if I go into labor during rush hour. 

The healthcare professionals, they’re for the "what if" emergencies. I need them for the medicine, the monitor, and the clinical safety, but they’re playing a different game. They see the physical, I see the reality. My informal support network fills in the gaps that the clinic doesn't even know exist—the loneliness, the logistical panic, the sheer physical struggle of existing in this house while seven months pregnant. 

I suppose I need both. I need the clinic to handle the medical side, but I *live* in the world my husband and neighbors share. They’re the ones who know exactly how many stairs I have to climb and how much that, alone, changes my day. They’re the ones who keep me human.
Can you describe a moment when informal support made a meaningful difference to your experience — what happened and why did it matter?

It was just a few days ago. I was standing at the bottom of our apartment stairs, looking up at them, and I just started crying. Not the quiet kind, but the shaky, sobbing kind where you can’t catch your breath. I was terrified about the baby, about the money, about the GBS test—it all just hit me at once. 

My neighbor, Sarah, saw me from her door. She didn’t ask what was wrong or tell me to "breathe through it" like a doctor might. She just came over, grabbed the heavy laundry basket I was struggling with, and walked up those stairs behind me, slow and steady, until we got to my door. She didn't say a word about my weight or the baby’s health. She just sat with me in the kitchen while I finished my tea, and she told me she’d be ready with her car the second I called her, no matter what time it was. 

That mattered because she took one "what if" off my plate. Knowing someone is watching the clock with me, knowing I don't have to face that climb or that commute alone—that’s the support that actually keeps me from spiraling. It was the difference between feeling like a patient waiting for a bad outcome and feeling like a mother being cared for.

When you think about your goals and expectations for your birth experience — what you hoped labour and delivery would be like — how clearly could you articulate those goals at the time, and how well did the care system support you in pursuing them?

I wish I could say I had some big, empowering birth plan, but the truth is, my only real goal is survival—mine and the baby's. I want to get through this, have them place the baby in my arms, and know we’re both safe. With six previous deliveries, I know how fast things can change, so I don't set my heart on a certain kind of delivery or a specific room. I’ve learned that the harder you cling to a "vision," the more it hurts when reality takes a sharp turn.

But even with my lowered expectations, the system makes it hard to feel like I have any control at all. They see me as a body that’s done this before, so they don’t bother explaining things. They assume I "know the drill." I wanted a space where I could say, "I’m terrified about the GBS and the timing of the antibiotics," and have them actually walk through a plan with me that respects my home life in Somerville. 

Instead, it feels like I’m being ushered into a factory line. I can’t articulate my goals because I’m too busy trying to keep up with the clinical requirements they’re piling on me. I’m not pursuing a "birth experience," I’m just trying to survive the logistics of getting to the hospital without falling apart.

When you think about the decisions you were asked to make during labour and delivery — around pain management, interventions, and procedures — how well-equipped did you feel to participate in those decisions in the moment?

That’s the part that terrifies me the most—being asked to make a big decision when the contractions are coming fast and my brain is just screaming. In those moments, I don't feel like a person with choices; I feel like a cornered animal. 

With my previous labors, there were times the doctors or nurses would throw a bunch of medical options at me—do I want this drug, or that procedure, or should we monitor this way? When you’re in active labor, you aren't thinking about the long-term impact of an intervention. You’re just looking for relief. It’s hard to feel "well-equipped" to participate when you’re literally in survival mode. The system assumes that because I’ve been through it before, I have this reservoir of calm, logical thinking, but that’s not how it works at all. I’m exhausted, I’m scared, and my focus is entirely on the baby.

Most of the time, I just end up saying "whatever you think is best." And that bothers me afterwards. I feel like I gave away my agency because I was too tired or too intimidated to fight for something else. I want to be involved, but I need them to explain things *before* the labor hits, in a way that doesn't feel like they're rushing me. I just want to feel like a partner, not a passenger, in my own delivery.
Can you describe a decision that was presented to you during labour — how was it explained, how much time did you have, and how did you feel making it?

With my third—or was it my fourth?—they started talking about an induction because I was, according to them, "stalling out." I was in that haze where the pain is everywhere at once, and suddenly there’s a doctor, eyes on a clipboard, telling me about Pitocin. They laid it out like it was just the next logical step to get things moving. 

They didn't give me time. They made it sound like if I didn't say yes right then, I was putting the baby at risk of exhaustion or distress. I didn't feel like I was making a choice; I felt like I was being herded. I was so exhausted I could barely speak, and my husband was just as terrified as I was. We both just nodded, because who are we to argue with a doctor who says the baby needs help?

Looking back, I wonder if I really needed it, or if they were just on a schedule to clear the room. I felt completely powerless. I wasn't a partner; I was a body that needed to be "fixed" so the delivery could finish. That’s the feeling that stays with me—that I’m just a set of parts in their machine, and the machine doesn't care if I’m ready or not. It’s why I’m so anxious about the GBS this time. I’m scared they’ll corner me again.

When you think about the emotional experience of your labour and delivery — from the very beginning through to the moment your baby was born — what were the most powerful feelings, and were you able to process them in the birth environment?

The most powerful feeling during birth isn't the pain—it’s the crushing sense of vulnerability. When I’m in that room, exposed and exhausted, I feel like I’m standing on the edge of a cliff. I’m waiting for the baby to arrive, but I’m also holding my breath, waiting to see if everything I’ve been told is going to work out right. 

In that environment, there’s no room for processing. It’s all about the monitors, the vitals, and the next contraction. If I start to cry from the sheer, overwhelming intensity of it, someone just tells me, "You’re doing great, just focus." It’s meant to be encouraging, but it feels like a silencing. They’re trying to keep the "clinical" environment stable, so they don’t want the "emotional" messiness that comes with being a person in pain.

I don't think I’ve ever been able to process the feelings *in* the room. I’m too busy trying to perform the role of a "good" patient so they’ll treat me with respect. I go through the motions, I follow the instructions, and I keep the fear locked behind my teeth until I’m home. It’s only days later, when I’m finally sitting in my own chair, that the weight of what just happened finally hits me. By then, it’s too late to talk about it with anyone who was actually there.
Can you take me to a specific moment — what was happening, what did you feel, and who was with you?

I remember with my fifth, the hospital was packed. I’d been there for hours, the lights were buzzing, and the staff kept rotating in and out like a revolving door. I was in the middle of a contraction—the kind that makes you see stars—and I was just pleading for a moment to breathe, to just sit up and try to find a rhythm.

A new nurse came in, checking her watch, and she told me I needed to stay flat because the monitor wasn't catching the baby’s heart rate correctly. She didn’t look at my face; she just adjusted the belts with these cold, clinical hands. I felt so small, like I was just a piece of furniture in the room. My husband was there, but he looked just as intimidated as I was; he kept apologizing to the nurse for me being in pain, like my suffering was an inconvenience.

I felt completely untethered. It wasn't just the pain; it was the realization that I had no say in how my body was being handled. I remember looking at the ceiling, thinking, *Is this how I bring my child into the world? Being talked over while I'm being held down?* It’s that exact memory that makes my chest tighten every time I think about the hospital now. I don't want to be "processed" again.

When you think about the behaviour and communication of your midwives, doctors, and care team during your birth, what interactions stood out — positively or negatively — and what made them significant?

The moments that stood out weren't the big, high-tech interventions. They were the small, quiet things. I remember one nurse, back with my second—she actually pulled a chair up next to the bed during a long, slow stretch. She didn't have her clipboard in her lap, and she didn't look at the monitor every ten seconds. She just held my hand, looked me in the eye, and asked, "How are you holding up, really?" It was so simple, but it felt like she’d dropped a lifeline. That mattered because it made me feel human, not like a chore.

On the flip side, the negatives are almost always about being talked *at* or talked *over*. It’s the doctors who walk in like they own the room, throwing out medical jargon like it’s a language I should already know. When they act like I’m an interruption to their shift instead of a person in the most vulnerable moment of my life, it sticks. It’s that dismissive tone—the "everything is fine, stop worrying" type of response—that hits hardest. It tells me that my concerns don’t actually matter to them. I don't need them to hold my hand the whole time, but I do need them to treat my fear with the same seriousness they treat the clinical data. Why is that so hard to get?

When you think about the physical environment of the place where you gave birth — the facilities, the equipment, the space — how did that environment affect your comfort, safety, and overall experience of birth?

The hospital room always feels like a cold, bright stage where I’m the only one who doesn’t have a script. Everything is gray or sterile white, and it’s full of these sharp, beeping machines that seem to have more authority than I do. When I’m lying there on that bed, the fluorescent lights reflecting off the metal equipment, I feel like an object being inspected rather than a mother bringing a life into the world.

There’s no comfort in that space. It’s all functionality—the bed doesn't adjust the way I need it to, the monitors are constantly tethering me to one spot, and there’s no privacy to just be in my own skin. It’s the opposite of "safe" in my mind. Every beep makes my heart race, and every time someone walks in and out, I feel exposed. 

I’ve had to birth in these spaces six times, and it’s always the same: I’m trying to find a rhythm, trying to find some softness, but the room is screaming "emergency" and "hurry up" at me from every corner. If I had a quiet, dim, private space—somewhere that felt like a *place* rather than a procedure room—I think I could actually focus on the labor instead of just trying to survive the environment.
What specific aspects of the physical environment helped or hindered you during labour and delivery?

The lights. They’re the first thing I notice every time. They’re always so clinical, so bright—like an interrogation room. It’s hard to let go and get into the rhythm of labor when there’s this harsh, buzzing white light shining directly down on you. I remember wanting, more than anything, to just dim them, to have some privacy and warmth, but there was always some nurse or resident coming in, flipping a switch, or adjusting a monitor that needed "better visibility." It made me feel like I was on display, not just birthing a baby.

The bed itself is another piece of it. It’s never meant for comfort; it’s meant for their access. It’s stiff, narrow, and always seems to be in the way when I’m trying to find a position that actually helps with the back pain. And then there are the wires. Being hooked up to all those machines—the constant beeping, the feeling that if I move, something is going to fall off or alarm—it makes me feel like a prisoner to the equipment. I couldn't move freely, couldn't pace, couldn't find a way to make the pain manageable because the environment dictated every move I made. It’s like the room was built for their efficiency, not for my body.

When you think about any complications, unexpected events, or emergency interventions during your birth, how were these communicated to you, and how did you experience the shift from a planned to an unplanned situation?

The shift is always like a sudden drop in the stomach. One minute you’re trying to manage the pain, and the next, the energy in the room completely changes. Everything gets tighter, faster, and louder. Usually, the first sign isn't a conversation—it's the nurses rushing in, the machines starting to beep in a different, frantic way, and the doctor’s face going tight.

They rarely *explain* it to me as a person. They give me the "emergency" version: "Baby’s heart rate dropped, we need to do X right now." It’s all directives. There’s no "this is what’s happening, here is why we’re worried, what do you think?" It’s just "sign here" or "we’re doing this because we have to." 

It’s terrifying. I’ve been there—that moment where you feel like you’ve completely lost the steering wheel. I don’t feel like I’m participating in a medical decision; I feel like I’m being swept away by a current. When I’ve had those complications, I’ve felt completely paralyzed. I want them to look at me, use plain language, and give me just five seconds to catch my breath before they start moving me around. I know they’re trying to save the baby, and I’m so grateful for that, but the way they steamroll over me in those moments makes me feel like I don't exist. It leaves me feeling fractured, like I was just a bystander to my own birth.

It’s hard to put into words, but there's this specific kind of grief that comes after those emergency moments. You're left with the baby, thank God, but you're also left with the memory of being handled like luggage. 

I think about the aftermath, once the room finally goes quiet. The adrenaline is still humming in my ears, my hands are shaking, and I'm staring at the ceiling tiles, trying to piece together what just happened. The nurses are usually bustling about, tidying up, clicking their pens, and moving on to the next chart. They don't look back to ask, "That was incredibly intense, how are you feeling about what just happened?" They see a successful birth—a "healthy outcome"—and they close the book on the trauma that got us there.

It makes me feel like I’m supposed to just be grateful and silent. If I say I’m upset or that I felt scared or sidelined, it’s like I’m being ungrateful for the baby being healthy. But both things can be true, right? I can be overjoyed for my child and still feel hurt by how I was treated. 

I’m dreading the possibility of that happening again. With this being my seventh, I feel like my body is being watched more closely, like they’re waiting for something to go wrong. I just want to be able to trust that, if things go sideways, I won't just be an "emergency case" to them. I want to be *me*. Is that really asking for too much? Just to be treated like a person, even when things are falling apart?
Who first raised the idea of trying to conceive, and how did those early conversations unfold?

Honestly, it wasn’t some big, formal conversation where we sat down and weighed the pros and cons on a piece of paper. It was… well, life. We’re married, we’re together, and it just sort of happened. 

With seven pregnancies, you stop looking at it like a "plan" and start looking at it like a reality of your life. When I found out this time, my husband and I had a long, quiet night in the kitchen. We were looking at our bills, looking at the cracks in the ceiling, and we both felt that same sharp, familiar spike of panic—*Can we really do this again?* 

But then there was that other thing, too. That feeling of, *We’ve made it through six, we can make it through one more.* It wasn't a choice made in luxury; it was a choice made in the middle of our struggle. We talked about how we’d manage the money, how we’d squeeze another crib into this drafty apartment, and how we’d just have to be twice as tough as we were last time. 

It was less "let's have a baby" and more "okay, this is happening, let’s hold on tight." We’ve always been a team, he and I. But I’d be lying if I said there wasn't a part of me that was just plain terrified that this might be the one that finally breaks us.

It’s funny, when you’re thirty-two and already have a house full of noise and needs, "planning" feels like a luxury for other people. For us, it’s always been about making space where there isn’t any. 

When that first positive test came, the feeling wasn’t just joy—it was a heavy sort of surrender. I looked at the kitchen, at the piles of laundry, at the shoes by the door, and I just thought, *Where is this little one going to fit?* My husband, he’s a good man, but I saw it in his face too. That flick of worry, the way he looks at the paycheck and then at me, wondering how we’re going to stretch things even thinner. 

We didn't talk about nurseries or names at first. We talked about how many more shifts he could pick up or if I could keep working a little longer without the swelling getting too bad. It’s hard to have the romantic conversations people on television have when you’re worried about the rent. But even with the worry, there’s this stubborn, quiet love. We’re in this together, climbing those stairs, facing the GBS, and everything else. It feels like every child we have is a fight we’re winning, even if the world around us makes it feel like we’re losing. I just want this next chapter to be the one where we finally get a bit of a break.
Were there aspects of your physical self that you felt proud of, anxious about, or uncertain about in relation to pregnancy?

It’s a complicated relationship I have with my body these days. Part of me is just… in awe. I look at my belly and think about how my body is growing this person from scratch, especially at thirty-two with six others already having taken their toll. There’s a quiet, fierce pride in that. I’m a vessel for life, and despite the drafts in this apartment, the tight budget, and the constant, crushing exhaustion, my body keeps showing up. It’s tough. It’s resilient. I try to hold onto that when I’m staring at the mirror.

But the anxiety? That’s always right there, sitting on top of the pride. I’m deeply uncertain about whether my body can handle the *stress* of this one. Every time I get a sharp cramp or a backache that lingers a bit too long, I get terrified that I’m reaching my breaking point—that I’ve pushed my system one time too many. I worry that my age and the sheer frequency of these pregnancies are showing. I feel like my body is a worn-out map where every stretch mark and ache tells a story of survival, but I’m scared the next chapter will be the one where the map finally tears. It’s a strange thing to feel so strong and so fragile all at the same time.

It's a strange feeling, being so aware of every stretch in my skin and every extra pound I’m carrying, because I know what it’s actually costing me. When people say, "you’re doing so well," it’s like they’re praising a car for running despite a broken engine. I’m proud of my body—I really am—but I’m also tired of it being treated like it’s disposable.

Sometimes, when I’m sitting in the clinic, I see these other women who look… lighter, I guess. Like their pregnancies are just a temporary event in their lives, not a permanent state of survival. I look at my own hands, my tired eyes, the way my feet swell after a double shift, and I feel a bit of shame, like I’m "showing" the struggle too much. 

I want my body to be seen as more than just a host for this baby. I want someone to acknowledge that this physical transformation is taking everything I have. I’m holding my breath until the birth, just waiting to see if my body can do it one more time without breaking. It’s hard to be proud when you’re constantly holding your breath, scared that the next step, the next shift, or the next contraction is the one that forces you to admit you’ve reached your limit. I just hope my body is as strong as everyone says it is.
Were there moments where you felt a healthcare professional spoke to you in a way that felt dismissive or condescending?

It happens more than I care to admit. It’s that tone—that specific, sugar-coated "oh, honey" voice they use when they think I don’t know what I’m talking about or when they want to brush off a question that doesn’t fit into their five-minute slot.

I remember a few weeks back, I asked about the potential for early intervention if my labor moves as fast as it did with my fourth. The doctor didn't even look at me; she just patted my hand, chuckled, and said, "Eugenie, you’re an old pro at this! You’ve done it six times, you’ll know what to do when the time comes."

She made it sound like my experience was a shortcut for her, a way to skip the part where she actually has to listen to my concerns. It was patronizing. Being a "pro" doesn't mean I’m not scared, and it certainly doesn't mean I’m immune to complications. It made me feel like my voice was just noise she had to wait to stop so she could get on with her paperwork. It’s infuriating to be treated like I’m some kind of factory machine that doesn't need to be spoken to with respect. I’m not just a "multi-para" or a chart on the wall—I’m a woman who is doing something dangerous and hard, and I deserve to be heard.

It’s like they have this mental checklist, and because I’ve been through the "seven pregnancies" stage, they assume they don’t need to explain the human side anymore. They skip straight to the clinical directives. If I say I’m worried about the pain or the logistics, they immediately pivot to, "Well, you know how it goes, you’ve been through it before." 

It feels like they’re weaponizing my history against me. My experience isn't a badge of honor in those rooms; it's a reason for them to be less attentive. It makes me feel incredibly isolated, because I’m standing there, feeling just as overwhelmed as I did the first time, but I’m expected to act like I’ve got it all under control. 

The condescension is in the way they look at their watches, the way they talk over me, or the way they explain things like I’m a child who doesn't understand her own body. It’s exhausting to have to fight to be taken seriously. I’m not asking for extra attention because I’m needy; I’m asking because this is *my* body, *my* baby, and *my* life we’re talking about. When they brush me off, it’s not just rude—it’s dangerous. It makes me shut down, and then I’m not asking the questions I need to ask, and that just feeds the anxiety even more.
Did older female relatives, such as your mother or grandmother, share advice or stories that influenced how you thought about getting pregnant?

My mother and grandmother… they were cut from a different cloth, I suppose. They were the kind of women who lived by the rule that you just get on with it. There wasn't much room for complaining in their generation. When I was younger, I’d hear them talk about their pregnancies like they were just another chore, like scrubbing the floor or putting dinner on the table. "You have your children, you do your work, you keep the house going," my grandmother would say. 

I grew up thinking that was the only way to be. That being a mother meant being a wall—strong, unmoving, and quiet about your own pain. So, in a way, they taught me to be tough, but they also taught me to hide when I’m scared. 

But sometimes, when I’m feeling particularly low, I think about them and I feel a bit of bitterness. Did they feel this heavy, too? Did they worry about how they’d get to the hospital, or if the baby would be okay, while they were busy working their hands raw? I suspect they did, but they never let it show. I carry that same weight, and I feel like I have to keep it hidden, just like they did. It’s hard, though. I want to be strong for my kids, but I’m tired of having to be a wall.

I think about my mother sometimes, and the image of her just standing there, doing the laundry or cleaning with a belly that was constantly growing—it just makes me feel a different kind of tired. She never let on that she was afraid. She’d just look at me, give me a little smile, and say, "Don’t you worry, the baby will come when it’s ready, and you’ll know what to do." 

But now that I’m here, thirty-two, seven months along with my seventh, I realize that silence—that "tough it out" approach—is a double-edged sword. It kept the house moving, sure, but it meant she was carrying all those worries about infections and money and complications completely alone. I feel like I’m walking in her footsteps, but the world is different now. The costs are higher, the hospitals are more crowded, and the stakes feel so much more visible.

I love them, I really do, but that "don't complain, just do it" advice feels like a heavy coat I’ve been wearing my whole life. Sometimes I just want to put it down. I want to be able to say, "I am terrified," without it sounding like I’m weak. I don't want to just be another woman who suffered in silence because that’s what was expected of us. I want my experience to mean more than just 'surviving it'.

I think the hardest part is that I want to tell my kids that it’s okay to be scared, even when you’re doing something "normal." My grandmother would have laughed at that. She’d say, "You’re doing what you were made for, stop your fussing." But that "fussing" is just me trying to hold onto my sanity. 

When I look at my own children now, I don't want them to think that being a mother means disappearing into the background, or that your fears and your physical pain are just invisible costs you have to pay. I want them to know that it’s okay to ask for help, and that it shouldn’t be a fight to be heard when you’re in pain or feeling overwhelmed. 

Being this deep into my seventh pregnancy, I’m realizing that my legacy shouldn’t be just "I endured it." I want it to be that I reached out, that I questioned things, and that I tried to make it better for the next time. It’s hard to break those old habits—the ones where I just nod and say "everything is fine"—but I’m trying. I’m trying for me, and I’m trying for them. If I can just get through this seventh time and be a bit more honest about what it’s actually like, maybe that’s a different kind of strength.
Can you describe a specific situation where you felt judged, overlooked, or not treated as a capable decision-maker regarding your reproductive health?

It’s the way they look at my chart—six previous births—and then at my clothes, which aren't new, and my hands, which are calloused from work. I feel like they have a script already written for me before I even open my mouth.

A few weeks ago, I tried to ask a specific question about whether I could skip one of the routine growth scans because it meant taking another half-day of unpaid leave, which we honestly couldn't afford. The way the doctor sighed—it wasn't just a sign of being busy, it was like a sign of annoyance, like I was being "difficult" or "negligent" for putting money before the scan. She didn’t ask why I was hesitating; she just gave me that look that says, *Some people just don't take care of themselves.*

I felt so small and so ashamed. I’m doing everything I can to keep this family afloat, and here she is, looking at me like I’m irresponsible. She didn't offer a compromise or try to work around my schedule; she just made me feel like my life circumstances were a burden to her efficiency. I left that room feeling like I wasn't a mother trying to protect her family’s future, but a patient who was failing to follow orders. It’s hard to feel like a capable decision-maker when the doctor treats your reality like a mistake you’re making.

It’s not just the doctor’s look, it’s the way the whole system seems designed for people who have nothing else to do but show up whenever they’re told. They don't realize that for me, every single appointment is a negotiation. Do I lose half a day of pay? Do I find someone to watch the other six kids? Do I pay for the bus, or do I walk and risk my back locking up? 

When I brought up the scan, I wasn't being "neglectful." I was doing math. I was trying to figure out if that scan was worth the groceries for the next three days. And when she dismissed me with that look—that sharp, pitying glance—it stripped away my agency. It made my decision feel like a moral failing rather than a practical necessity of my life.

I walked out of that clinic feeling like I’d been scolded. It makes you realize that to them, "good care" is just checking off their boxes, and if your real life gets in the way of those boxes, you’re the problem. I’m thirty-two, I’ve brought six lives into this world, and I know exactly what I’m doing when it comes to keeping my family together. Being treated like I’m a child who needs a lecture is the last thing I have the energy for. I just want them to work *with* me, not against me.
Did you have reliable and private access to a smartphone or computer throughout your pregnancy, and did that change at any point?

My phone is... well, it’s a lifeline, but it’s an old, cracked thing that barely holds a charge half the time. It’s definitely not "reliable" in the way some people have it. It’s a bit of a relic, but it’s all I’ve got. 

When I’m at home, I have a beat-up laptop that I use mostly for picking up extra work or keeping track of the household bills. It’s not exactly private, either, with all the kids running around and needing things every five minutes. There’s no space in this apartment where I can just sit and be alone with my thoughts—or my worries—without someone asking for a snack or needing me to help with homework. 

I’ve looked up the GBS stuff on my phone, late at night when everyone is finally asleep, but I have to be careful. If the screen light wakes someone up, that’s it—my moment is over. And honestly, it’s not really "private" because I’m always worried about what I might see, what might pop up in a search, or if I’ll accidentally click on something that’ll just make my anxiety worse. It’s hard to have any privacy when you’re living in a house full of people, all competing for the same limited space and time. It’s just another thing I have to navigate in silence.

It’s funny, the way people talk about the "digital age" of medicine—like having an app or a portal is some kind of magical bridge between the clinic and home. For me, that technology feels like a wall. They tell me to log into the patient portal to see my lab results or to message the midwife, but they don't stop to think about whether I *can*.

When I’m at the clinic, they’re always saying, "Check the app, check the portal." But my phone is old, the screen is cracked, and half the time it won’t load the pages correctly. And even if it did, who has the time to navigate through all those drop-down menus while I’m in the middle of a double shift or trying to get the kids fed? 

It’s like they assume everyone is sitting in a quiet office with a high-speed connection, waiting for a notification. My "digital access" is just one more source of stress. Sometimes I feel like if I don't check that portal, they think I’m not "participating" in my care. But I’m busy living in the real world, not the one on the screen. It just makes me feel further behind—like I’m missing some secret language that everyone else is using to get better care than I am.
Were there moments when a healthcare provider dismissed or minimised a concern you raised about your body or a symptom you were experiencing, and how did that make you feel?

It’s the phrase "everything looks normal" that gets me. It’s like a wall they put up. A few weeks back, I was having these sharp, radiating pains in my lower back—different from the usual ache—and I mentioned it during an appointment because I was genuinely worried it was more than just the pregnancy weight.

The midwife didn't even put down her pen. She just gave a quick, breezy nod and said, "That’s just part of the territory with number seven, Eugenie. Everything looks normal on the scan, so don’t you worry about it." 

That made me feel like I was invisible. It felt like she was telling me my own body was lying to me, or worse, that I was just being dramatic. I wasn't asking for a miracle cure; I was asking to be *heard*. When they minimize a symptom like that, it makes me doubt myself. If I bring it up again, am I the "difficult" patient? If I push, do I look like I’m wasting their time? It isolates you. You walk out of that office carrying your pain, and you have to wonder if you’re actually okay or if you’re just being ignored until something truly breaks. It’s a lonely place to be when you’re already so scared for this baby.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I’ve been sitting here thinking about what I’ve said, and I suppose there's one thing. Everyone talks about the "maternity care experience" like it’s just the hours you spend in the clinic or the delivery room. But the care I’m getting doesn’t stop at the clinic door—it follows me home. 

The weight of it is constant. I keep going back to that GBS swab. For them, it’s a quick clinical task. For me, it’s a terrifying question of *what if*. *What if I’m positive? What if the bus is late? What if I’m not "pro" enough to handle the stress of the antibiotics?* 

I just want the people in charge of my care to understand that when they talk to me, they’re talking to someone whose entire life—my job, my kids, my exhaustion, my commute—is sitting right there on that exam table with me. I don’t want them to fix my life, I know they can’t. I just want them to look at the whole picture instead of just the "medium risk" file.

I’m tired of being a box to be checked. I’m a woman, I’m a mother, and I’m just trying to make sure this baby survives. If they could just see that—really see that—I think I’d be able to breathe a little easier. Thank you for listening. It… it actually helps to say it out loud.
